summary: Powerful Morning Dew, tight On‑The‑Road segue & high‑energy second set
blurb: The show delivers a smoking Morning Dew and a seamless On The Road Again → Beat It On Down The Line transition in the first set. A second‑set surge with Estimated Prophet → Eyes Of The World and an expansive Truckin' → The Other One jam keeps the momentum rolling. Overall performance is tight, melodic and full of 1982‑era vigor.
review: On October 9, 1982 at Frost Amphitheatre the Dead were in top form, opening with a rollicking Alabama Getaway that quickly settled into a flawless first set. Highlights include an immaculate On The Road Again → Beat It On Down The Line segue, a bright West L.A. Fadeaway and a truly smoking Morning Dew where Garcia’s lead work was described as “powerful and emotionally charged.”  The second set builds relentlessly – Throwing Stones segues straight into Touch of Grey before launching into one of the era’s classic Estimated Prophet → Eyes Of The World stretches, followed by a thunderous Drums > Space leading into a soaring Truckin' → The Other One marathon that culminates in an uplifting Morning Dew and Saturday Night encore.  Across all recordings the band sounds tight: Phil’s bass lines lock in especially during the deep dissonant passages, Brent adds texture with his electric piano, while Mickey and Bill drive the groove with “strong” drumming.  Despite a few mix quirks (Bob’s rhythm guitar low on one SBD), the performance is consistently praised as energetic, adventurous, and quintessentially 1982.

rating_calculation.sentiment_rationale: The majority of recordings (9 out of 11) carry a strongly positive sentiment, repeatedly praising the same standout moments – Morning Dew, On The Road Again → Beat It On Down The Line, Estimated Prophet → Eyes Of The World and the Truckin' → The Other One jam. No consistent negative comments were found, only two mixed notes about mix balance. This consensus warrants a high positive adjustment.

best_recording.reason: Clean soundboard capture with good overall balance; limited hiss and only minor mix quirks, making it the most reliable source for hearing the show’s nuances.

key_highlights: Smoking Morning Dew with Garcia’s soaring lead work
Seamless On The Road Again → Beat It On Down The Line segue
Estimated Prophet → Eyes Of The World sequence that drives second‑set momentum
Truckin' → The Other One marathon delivering expansive improvisation
song_highlights: Beat It On Down The Line

band_performance.Jerry: Garcia delivers powerful, emotionally charged leads on Morning Dew and rides the high‑energy jams with a Dylan‑channeled tone and adventurous improvisation throughout.
band_performance.Phil: Lesh’s bass is solid and often highlighted – from the smiling GSET opener to the deep, dissonant passages in the second set – locking in tightly despite occasional mix low‑end issues.
band_performance.Bob: Weir’s vocals are strong and his rhythm guitar shines on most tracks; a few recordings note his guitar lower in the mix, but overall he adds texture and humor with quirky vocal moments.
band_performance.Brent: Mydland’s electric piano provides rich texture across the set; occasional patch‑jump glitches are noted, yet his support work and vocal pushes (e.g., on Deal) remain solid.
band_performance.Mickey: Hart delivers strong, unobtrusive drumming that powers the jams, especially during the explosive Drums > Space segment.
band_performance.Billy: Kreutzmann’s drumming pairs with Mickey to give a deep, well‑separated stereo presence and drives the rhythmic foundation of the extended second‑set jams.

must_listen_sequences: Beat It On Down The Line
